Dear Mr. Dorsey:
we have received your letter of March 28, L991, in r'rhich you
seek advice on behalf of Douglas Hinchliffe, a member of the
Rancho Palos Verdes city council and RedeveLopment Agency,
regarding his duties and responsibilities under the conflict-of-

of the potiticat Reform Act ("the Act").1

advice as Mr. Hinchliffe's authorized representat j' ve .
The follolving advice is based upon the facts provided in your
Ietter of March 2a, L99L and in a subsequent telephone
conversation on April 4, L991. while it is understood from your
Letter and the tel.ephone conversation that various governmental
decisions are both occurring and forthcomj.ng, no specific pending
decision was referenced in your Ietter. 
Therefore, the advj-ce
provideo in this Letter is informal only.2
OUEST ION
A member of the c j-ty's council and redevelopment agency lives
in a landslide abatement district that is adjacent to two other
Iandslide abatement districts. 
As a result of lj.tigation
settlements the council and agency wil-I be making decisions to
both implement the settlement agreements as weII as to determine

specific landslide abatement prcjects in the two adjacent

May the council and agency member participate in these

COIiCLUSION
The council- and agency member may participate in decisions to
implement the settlelflent and to undertake specific IandsLj.de
abatement projects provided the decisions wiII not have a

FACTS
Three landslides pertinent to this inquiry occurred in 1955
( "Portugese Bendr') , L978 ("Abalone coverr) and 1980 ("KLondike
canyon't) in or near the jurisdictj.onal boundaries of the city of
Rancho Palos verdes (r'the city"), which was incorporated in 1973.
The Portugese Bend landslide was found to have been created by the
county of Los Angeles ("the county") as a resul-t of roadway
construction. The geographic location of this landslide is
between the other tvro: the Abalone Cove located to the west and
the Klondike canyon located to the east.
Following the Klondike Canyon tandslide in 1980, the city
established two landslide abatement districts, 
the Abalone cove
Landslide Abatement District and the Klondike canyon LandsLide
Abatement District, 
to construct and maintain improvements
designed to stop the landslides. The city imposed a buiJ-dl'ng
moraiorium to prohibit constructj.on in the landslide areas. The
city also formed a redevelopment agency ('rthe agency"),
conitituted by the city council, and all properties impacted by
the landslides were included by the aqency in a specific project
area. The county and several honeov/ners ("Friends of the Bendrr)
sued the city to invalidate the project area. other homeowners
sued the city and county for the damages caused by the landslides.
Ultimately, the Iitigation 
was mostly settLed. The
settlement provided that the county would dismiss its action
against the formation of the proje-t area,3 would form an
improvement district 
in the Abalone Cove area to fund construction
of abatement improvements, and would purchase bonds in the amount
of S10,oo0,ooo (secured by J.iens on the properties included within
the assessnent district) 
and accePt payment of tax increment by
the agency to reduce the assessnents. The county also agreed to
establish a second assessment district in the Portugese Bend area
and purchase S5,000,000 of improvement bonds to construct
Iandslide abatement irnprovements in that area, upon cornpletion of
all Abalone Cove improvements.
' 
Th. homeowners' group - "Friends of the
set:Ie and the matter ',rent to triai without
plaintiff. 
The city prevailed at trial 
and
appealed the decis ion.
Bend" - refused to
the county as a
the homeowners have

The Abalone cove LandsLide PaneI of Experts, a seven member
grcup of experts designated in the settlement agreement, uas
f oned to approve expenditures fron the ten million dollar bond
funds. At least five of the seven members of the panel are
required to approve any expenditure. The panel directs the city
to build what is necessary, and in what order. S1.5 million is
always earmarked and spent. A similar panel of experts for the
Portugese Bend area is or wiII also be constituted for decisions
concerning expenditure of the $5 million in improvement bonds.
The county forned the Abalone cove Assessment District in
December, 1990. The agency, required by the terns of the
settlement to designate a portion of j.ts tax increment revenues to
the retirement of the Abalone cove bonds, announced that it would
commit aLI tax increment revenues it received for such purpose.
Agency projections indicate that sufficient funds wj.II be
avaifinle to retire the Abalone Cove bonds, but that it was
unlikely that funds to retire the Portugese Bend bonds vould be
avaj-Iab1e for at least twenty years. It was therefore unJ-ikely
that the Portugese Bend bonds would be issued within that tine, if
at all.
Mr. Hinchliffe owns his hone in the Portugese Bend club (rrthe
club,,). The club's homeowners' association includes aII area
property owners and owns the streets and the beach in the club.
ihe location of Mr. Hinchliffe's property subiects the property to
the city-inposed development noratorium- AdditionallY, Mr.
Hinchliffe's property j.s Iocated
(a) more than 3600 feet f rori the eastern-most (and closest)
boundary of the Abalone Cove landslide;
(b) approximately 600 feet from the nearest boundary of the
Portugese Bend I ands I ide,'
(c) vrithin the boundaries of the Klondike canyon Landslide
Abatement District; 
and
(d) within the boundaries of the agency's project area.
You have indicated that Mr. Hinchliffe abstains from
participating in city and agency decisions concerning the
iortugese Bend landsLide. You also believe that decisions
concerning the abaternent of the Abalone cove landslide wiIl not
Iikely have a foreseeable material financial effect on the value
of Mr. Hinchliffe's residence. Nevertheless, Mr. Hinchliffe has
also abstained from participating in Abalone cove landslide
decisions because the terns of the litigation 
settlement provide
that the issuance of the Portugese Bend bonds requires the agency
to expend the Slo rnillion in Abalone cove bonds as a prerequisite.

Page 4
ANALYS I S
A public official 
may not inake, participate in, or use his or
her official 
position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official 
knows or has reason to know he or she has a
financial interest. 
(Section 87100.) A public official 
has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a rnaterial financial- effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, 
a member of the official's 
irnmed j.ate family, or on any
real property in which the official 
has a direct or indirect
interLst-worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more. (Section
87103 (b) . )
Because he is an elected member of the Rancho Palos verdes
city council and also serves as a member of the city's
redevelopment agency, Mr' Hinchliffe is a public official.
(Section 82048.) Therefore, he must disqualify hinself from any
city council decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable
material f j.nancial. effect on hinr or on his real property interest
that is distinguishable from the public generally. 
(Sectj-on
87103 (b) . )
Foreseeable Financial Effect
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substanti.al likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not
required, but an effect that is rnereJ-y a possibility is.not
reisonably foreseeable. (Downev cares v. Downev communitv
Development Com. (1987) 196 Ca1.App.3d 983; In re Thorner (1975)
f nppC Ops. fgS.) The Act, however, does seek to prevent even the
appearante of a possible conflict of interest. 
(l'Jitt v. Morrow
(L977) 7O caI..qPP.3d 817 , 823 .)
The questions you have raised all pertain to Mr. Hinchl'if fe's
participa€ion in remedial decisions concerning Iands).ides in the
jurisaittion. 
To the extent such decisions result in land
;tabilization, 
j.t is reasonable to conclude that property values
in the jurisdiction wiIl be impacted positively,' to the extent
that su6h decj.sions do not accomplish the intended objectives, it
is reasonable to conclude that Property values in the jurisdiction
may decline. Therefore, the decisions appear to satisfy the
"rlasonably foreseeable" requirement of the Act's conflict of
interest provis ions .
}taterial Financial Effect
The Commission has adopted Regulation L8702.3 (copy enclosed)
in order to determine whether the foreseeable effect of a deci.sion
is material as to real. property in which an official 
has an
interest. 
You have informed us that Mr. Hinchliffe will be asked
to participate in decisions involving the implementation of the
Iitlgatj.on settlenent agreement. on the basis of the inforrnation


File No. I-91-201

you have provided, it appears that the decisions can be classified
into two categories, one involving the Abalone cove Iandslide area
and one involving the Portugese Bend landslide area. The Klondike
Canyon Iandslide area is not part of the Iitigation 
settlement.
Because I.!r. Hinchliffe's property is located approximately
3,600 feet from the nearest boundary of the Abalone Cove Landslide
area and approximately 600 feet fron the nearest boundary of the
Portugese Bend Iandslide area, analysis under Regulation 18702.3
is different for decisions pertaining to each landslj-de area.
Abalone Cove
For property located nore than 2,500 feet from the boundary
of the site of a proposed decision (Abalone Cove), the effect of
the decision wilI be considered naterial onlv if:
(1) There are specific circumstances
regarding the decj.sj.on, its effect, and the nature
of the real property in which the official 
has an
interest, r^/hich make it reasonabJ.y f oreseeable that
the fair market value or the rental value of the
real property in which the official 
has an interest
will be affected by the amounts set forth in
subdivisions (a) (3) (A) or (a) (3) (B)4; and
(2) Either of the following aPPIY:
(A) The effect wiIl. not be substantj.ally the
same as the effect upon at least 25 percent of a1I
the properties which are within a 2,500 foot radius
of the boundaries of the real property in which the
official 
has an interest; or
(B) There are not at }east 10 properties
under separate ownership within a 2,500 foot radj.us
of the property in whj.ch the official 
has an
interest.
Regulation 18702.3 (b) .
o 
(u) The effect of a decision is material as to real property
in which an official 
has a direct, indirect or beneficial
ownership interest (not including a leasehold interest) , if ...:
(3) The real property in which the official 
has an interest
is located outside a radius of 3oo feet and ... within a radius of
2,500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the decision and the decision
will have a reasonably foreseeable f inancj'aI effect of:
(A) Ten thousand dotlars (S1o,ooo) or more on the fair
market value of the real property in which the official 
has an
interest; or
(B) I,iill affect the rental value of the property by $1,000
or more per 12 month Period.

whether the effect of the decision on l"tr. Hinchliffe's property is
positive or negative is of no consequence under the Act. 
(Youno
Advice Letter, A-89-149, copy enclosed. )
Mr. Hinchliffe's participation in decisions concerning
Abalone cove would be permitted unless the value of his property
wiIl be affected by the doIlar amounts specified j,n Regulation
18702.3(b) and there are specific factors which make the impact on

rn orovidinq advice the commissi.on does not act as a finder of

which shaII be considered in determining
v/hether the decision wiII have the effects set
forth in subdivision (a) (s)(e) or (B) include,
but are not limited to:

is the subject of the decision and the
nagnitude of the proposed project or change in
use in relationship to the property in which
the official 
has an interest;
(2) whether it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision wiIl, affect the development
potential or income producing potential of the
propertY,'

reasonably foreseeable that the decision wiII
result in a change to the character of the
neighborhood including, but not limited to,
effects on traffic, 
view, privacy, intensity
of use, noise 1eve1s, air enj.ssions, or
sinilar traits of the neighborhood.
(Regulation L8702.3 (d) . )
After considering these factors, if you conclude that a
particular Abalone Cove decision wil.1 not impact Mr. Hinchliffe's
property's fair narket value by at least S10,000, or its rental
value by 51,ooo in a l2-month, Mr. Hinchliffe would be permitted
to participate in the decision. If you conclude that a financial

impact at these levels is likely to result, he is stil1 pernitted

on the basis of the facts presented, we believe this conflict
analysis to be appropriate for each separate Abalone cove

assessnent district 
bonds. If you have concluded, as your letter

Portugese Benal
For property located between 300 and 2,500 feet from the
boundary of the site of a proposed decision (Portugese Bend) , the
effect of tne decision will be considered material if it wiII have
a reasonabLy foreseeable financial effect of:
(A) Ten thousand dollars (S10,000) or nore on
the fair market value of the real, property in which
the official 
has an interest; or
5 
wh.th.r or not there are ten or nore properties under
separate ownership within 2,500 feet of your property (Section
t81o2.3 (b) (2) (B) is a simple factual determination. As
illustrative 
exarnples of how Secti.on 1a702.3 (b) (2) (A) is applied,
if Mr. Hinchliffe's property were the only parcel within a 2,500-
foot radius that would benefit fron a specific Abalone Cove
decision, he would be required to disqualify himself from
participatinq in the decision because less than the requisite
nunber of properties $/ithin a 2,5oo foot radius of his property
wouLd be affected by the decision in a manner si:nilar to his
property. Sinilarl-y, disqualificatj.on would be required if there
were ten parcels within a 2,500 foot radius of his property and
only one of the ten would be affected by the proposed Abal.one Cove
decision in a manner similar to his property. However, if there
were twenty properties within a 2,500 foot radius of his property
and at least five of the twenty would be affected by the proposed
Abalone Cove decision in a manner similar to the impact on his
property, Mr. Hinchliffe would then be permitted to particl-pate in
the proposed decis ion.

(B) will affect the rental value of the
property by Sr.000 or nore per 12 month period.
(Regulation ta1o2.3(a) (3) (A) and (B) .)
Again, whether the effect of the decision is positive or negative
is of no consequence under the Act.
l,tr. Hinchliffe's participation in decisions concerning
Portugese Bend lrould be permitted unless the val-ue of his property
wilI be affected by the do1lar anounts specified in Regulation
18702.3 (a) (3)(A) and (B). Again, this is a factual determination,
for which the criteria of Regulation 18702.3(d) shouLd be
re ferenced .
sequence of Decisions
Normally, every governmental decision is independently
analyzed with respect to foreseeabi).ity and materiality issues.
(In re or,ren (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. ?7; Heidt Advice Letter, A-90-245,
copies enclosed. ) However, decisions that are interrelated are
not considered separately. (Heidt; sweenv Advice Letter, A-89-
639; Miller Advice Letter, A-82-119, copies enclosed. )
Disqualification that is required on one interrelated decision
requires disqualification on the other interrelated decision as
wel]. 
(Scher Advj.ce Letter, A-88-479, copy enclosed. )
on the basis of the facts you have provided, it appears that
decisions to expend funds from the S10 nillion of Abalone Cove
assessment district 
bonds are required preconditions for any
expenditure of bond funds for Portugese Bend: untiL the $10
million has been expended, the 55 million !.ri11 not be purchased by
the county and therefore could not be expended by the city.
Ho$rever, you have also stated that because of existing
property values in the Portugese Bend area, proiections indicate
that there would not be sufficient revenue to retire the 55
million Portugese Bend bonds for at least twenty years. You have
indicated that it is unlikely that either the agency will cal} on
the county, or the city will take any action, to issue the bonds
absent a projected sufficient tax incrernent to retire the bonds.
Unless the property values increased at a rate substantially
exceeding what is projected, you believe it unlikely that the S5
million in bonds will be issued wj.thin the next twenty years, if
at all.
Although expenditure decisions concerning the Abalone cove
assessnent district 
bonds are required preconditions for any
subsequent expenditure of bond funds for Portugese Bend, we are
unable to conclude, on the basis of your representat ions , that the
Iinkage by itself is sufficient to appear to qualify decisions
concerning Abalone cove as constituting a foreseeable material


File No. I -91-2 01
Page 9
financial impact on Mr. Hinchliffe's property interest. 
The
uncertainty as to whether the Portugese Bend bonds will be issued,
the present speculativeness as to how the funds wiII be expended
if the bonds are issued, and the uncertainty whether a resulting
expenditure wil-I attain the financial threshoLds of Regulation
la702.3 (a) (3)(A) or (B) , collectively undernine the notion that
there is a rtsubstantial likelihoodrr that the effect of an Abalone
Cove decision wiII be reasonably foreseeable with respect to Mr.
Hinchliffe's property. 
(See Downev Cares, suDra; In re Thorner,
supra . ; 7
The trPublic Generallyrr Exception
If it is your conclusion that the specified financial impact
and other requisites as provided for in Regulation 18702.3 are
present with respect to a decision concerning Abal-one cove or
Portugese Bend, a disqualifying conflict wouLd be present.
Neveriheless, Mr. Hinchliffe is stilf 
permitted to participate in
the decision in question if the effect of the decision on his
interests is not distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally. 
(Section 87103.) Although specifics in your letter
ire lacking, we can provide you with the following generaL advice.
The financial effect of the city counciL or agency decision
on Mr. Hinchliffe's property i.s distinguishable fron the effect on
the public AeneraIIy unless the decision's affect on his property
is substantially the same as it is on all members of the public or
a significant segment of the public. 
(Regulation 18703; Jorqensen
Advice Letter, No. A-90-017,' fn re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1; fn
re Owen (7976\ 2 FPPC Ops. 77. copies enclosed. )
The "public generally" is conprised of the entire
jurisdiction of the agency in question' (fn re Leqan, suDra.)
Because the agency in question is the ci.ty council and/or
redevelopment agency, the publj,c generally in this instance is the
entire city's population. Regulation 18703 also permits the
application of the 'rpublic generallyf exception when a decision
affects the official's 
interests in substantially the sane nanner
as it witl affect a significant segnent of the public. 
Those
facts indicating how, and whether, a specific decision concerning
Abalone cove or Portugese Bend will impact all, or a signj.ficant
' 
Th. existence of other facts not presented here regarding the
issuance of the Portugese Bend bonds nay change this conclusion.
Additionally, if other facts become available to you or Mr.
Hinchli.ffe which indi.cate that the Abalone Cove decision wi,I1 have
a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Mr. Hj,nchlif fe's
property interest, Regulation L8702.3 (b) , discussed abcve. would
apply to determine whether such effect is material.


FiLe No. r-91-2 01

segment, of thg city's residents uould required separate
consideration. a
Fina1ly, we have been inforrned by the city nanager that the
population of the city is approximately 41,00o, and that the
jurisdiction covers approximately thirteen square miIes.
Therefore, the publ.ic general exception provided for in Regulation
18703.1 (copy enclosed) is not applicable.
I hope this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this

sections.question: A member of the city's council and redevelopment agency lives in a landslide abatement district that is adjacent to two other landslide abatement districts. As a result of litigation settlements the council and agency will be making decisions to both implement the settlement agreements as well as to determine specific landslide abatement projects in the two adjacent districts. May the council and agency member participate in these decisions?
sections.conclusion: The council- and agency member may participate in decisions to implement the settlement and to undertake specific landslide abatement projects provided the decisions will not have a foreseeable material financial impact on his property interests.

sections.analysis:
A public official 
may not inake, participate in, or use his or
her official 
position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official 
knows or has reason to know he or she has a
financial interest. 
(Section 87100.) A public official 
has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a rnaterial financial- effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, 
a member of the official's 
irnmed j.ate family, or on any
real property in which the official 
has a direct or indirect
interLst-worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more. (Section
87103 (b) . )
Because he is an elected member of the Rancho Palos verdes
city council and also serves as a member of the city's
redevelopment agency, Mr' Hinchliffe is a public official.
(Section 82048.) Therefore, he must disqualify hinself from any
city council decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable
material f j.nancial. effect on hinr or on his real property interest
that is distinguishable from the public generally. 
(Sectj-on
87103 (b) . )
Foreseeable Financial Effect
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substanti.al likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not
required, but an effect that is rnereJ-y a possibility is.not
reisonably foreseeable. (Downev cares v. Downev communitv
Development Com. (1987) 196 Ca1.App.3d 983; In re Thorner (1975)
f nppC Ops. fgS.) The Act, however, does seek to prevent even the
appearante of a possible conflict of interest. 
(l'Jitt v. Morrow
(L977) 7O caI..qPP.3d 817 , 823 .)
The questions you have raised all pertain to Mr. Hinchl'if fe's
participa€ion in remedial decisions concerning Iands).ides in the
jurisaittion. 
To the extent such decisions result in land
;tabilization, 
j.t is reasonable to conclude that property values
in the jurisdiction wiIl be impacted positively,' to the extent
that su6h decj.sions do not accomplish the intended objectives, it
is reasonable to conclude that Property values in the jurisdiction
may decline. Therefore, the decisions appear to satisfy the
"rlasonably foreseeable" requirement of the Act's conflict of
interest provis ions .
}taterial Financial Effect
The Commission has adopted Regulation L8702.3 (copy enclosed)
in order to determine whether the foreseeable effect of a deci.sion
is material as to real. property in which an official 
has an
interest. 
You have informed us that Mr. Hinchliffe will be asked
to participate in decisions involving the implementation of the
Iitlgatj.on settlenent agreement. on the basis of the inforrnation
you have provided, it appears that the decisions can be classified
into two categories, one involving the Abalone cove Iandslide area
and one involving the Portugese Bend landslide area. The Klondike
Canyon Iandslide area is not part of the Iitigation 
settlement.
Because I.!r. Hinchliffe's property is located approximately
3,600 feet from the nearest boundary of the Abalone Cove Landslide
area and approximately 600 feet fron the nearest boundary of the
Portugese Bend Iandslide area, analysis under Regulation 18702.3
is different for decisions pertaining to each landslj-de area.
Abalone Cove
For property located nore than 2,500 feet from the boundary
of the site of a proposed decision (Abalone Cove), the effect of
the decision wilI be considered naterial onlv if:
(1) There are specific circumstances
regarding the decj.sj.on, its effect, and the nature
of the real property in which the official 
has an
interest, r^/hich make it reasonabJ.y f oreseeable that
the fair market value or the rental value of the
real property in which the official 
has an interest
will be affected by the amounts set forth in
subdivisions (a) (3) (A) or (a) (3) (B)4; and
(2) Either of the following aPPIY:
(A) The effect wiIl. not be substantj.ally the
same as the effect upon at least 25 percent of a1I
the properties which are within a 2,500 foot radius
of the boundaries of the real property in which the
official 
has an interest; or
(B) There are not at }east 10 properties
under separate ownership within a 2,500 foot radj.us
of the property in whj.ch the official 
has an
interest.
Regulation 18702.3 (b) .
o 
(u) The effect of a decision is material as to real property
in which an official 
has a direct, indirect or beneficial
ownership interest (not including a leasehold interest) , if ...:
(3) The real property in which the official 
has an interest
is located outside a radius of 3oo feet and ... within a radius of
2,500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the decision and the decision
will have a reasonably foreseeable f inancj'aI effect of:
(A) Ten thousand dotlars (S1o,ooo) or more on the fair
market value of the real property in which the official 
has an
interest; or
(B) I,iill affect the rental value of the property by $1,000
or more per 12 month Period.

whether the effect of the decision on l"tr. Hinchliffe's property is

(Youno

Mr. Hinchliffe's participation in decisions concerning

After considering these factors, if you conclude that a

impact at these levels is likely to result, he is stil1 pernitted

Portugese Benal
For property located between 300 and 2,500 feet from the

the official 
has an interest; or
5 
wh.th.r or not there are ten or nore properties under
separate ownership within 2,500 feet of your property (Section
t81o2.3 (b) (2) (B) is a simple factual determination. As
illustrative 
exarnples of how Secti.on 1a702.3 (b) (2) (A) is applied,
if Mr. Hinchliffe's property were the only parcel within a 2,500-
foot radius that would benefit fron a specific Abalone Cove
decision, he would be required to disqualify himself from

wouLd be affected by the decision in a manner si:nilar to his
property. Sinilarl-y, disqualificatj.on would be required if there
were ten parcels within a 2,500 foot radius of his property and
only one of the ten would be affected by the proposed Abal.one Cove
decision in a manner similar to his property. However, if there
were twenty properties within a 2,500 foot radius of his property
and at least five of the twenty would be affected by the proposed
Abalone Cove decision in a manner similar to the impact on his

the proposed decis ion.

(B) will affect the rental value of the
property by Sr.000 or nore per 12 month period.
(Regulation ta1o2.3(a) (3) (A) and (B) .)
Again, whether the effect of the decision is positive or negative
is of no consequence under the Act.
l,tr. Hinchliffe's participation in decisions concerning
Portugese Bend lrould be permitted unless the val-ue of his property
wilI be affected by the do1lar anounts specified in Regulation
18702.3 (a) (3)(A) and (B). Again, this is a factual determination,
for which the criteria of Regulation 18702.3(d) shouLd be
re ferenced .
sequence of Decisions
Normally, every governmental decision is independently
analyzed with respect to foreseeabi).ity and materiality issues.
(In re or,ren (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. ?7; Heidt Advice Letter, A-90-245,
copies enclosed. ) However, decisions that are interrelated are
not considered separately. (Heidt; sweenv Advice Letter, A-89-
639; Miller Advice Letter, A-82-119, copies enclosed. )
Disqualification that is required on one interrelated decision
requires disqualification on the other interrelated decision as
wel]. 
(Scher Advj.ce Letter, A-88-479, copy enclosed. )
on the basis of the facts you have provided, it appears that
decisions to expend funds from the S10 nillion of Abalone Cove
assessment district 
bonds are required preconditions for any
expenditure of bond funds for Portugese Bend: untiL the $10
million has been expended, the 55 million !.ri11 not be purchased by
the county and therefore could not be expended by the city.
Ho$rever, you have also stated that because of existing
property values in the Portugese Bend area, proiections indicate
that there would not be sufficient revenue to retire the 55
million Portugese Bend bonds for at least twenty years. You have
indicated that it is unlikely that either the agency will cal} on
the county, or the city will take any action, to issue the bonds
absent a projected sufficient tax incrernent to retire the bonds.
Unless the property values increased at a rate substantially
exceeding what is projected, you believe it unlikely that the S5
million in bonds will be issued wj.thin the next twenty years, if
at all.
Although expenditure decisions concerning the Abalone cove
assessnent district 
bonds are required preconditions for any
subsequent expenditure of bond funds for Portugese Bend, we are
unable to conclude, on the basis of your representat ions , that the
Iinkage by itself is sufficient to appear to qualify decisions
concerning Abalone cove as constituting a foreseeable material

financial impact on Mr. Hinchliffe's property interest. 
The
uncertainty as to whether the Portugese Bend bonds will be issued,
the present speculativeness as to how the funds wiII be expended
if the bonds are issued, and the uncertainty whether a resulting
expenditure wil-I attain the financial threshoLds of Regulation
la702.3 (a) (3)(A) or (B) , collectively undernine the notion that
there is a rtsubstantial likelihoodrr that the effect of an Abalone
Cove decision wiII be reasonably foreseeable with respect to Mr.
Hinchliffe's property. 
(See Downev Cares, suDra; In re Thorner,
supra . ; 7
The trPublic Generallyrr Exception
If it is your conclusion that the specified financial impact
and other requisites as provided for in Regulation 18702.3 are
present with respect to a decision concerning Abal-one cove or
Portugese Bend, a disqualifying conflict wouLd be present.
Neveriheless, Mr. Hinchliffe is stilf 
permitted to participate in
the decision in question if the effect of the decision on his
interests is not distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally. 
(Section 87103.) Although specifics in your letter
ire lacking, we can provide you with the following generaL advice.
The financial effect of the city counciL or agency decision
on Mr. Hinchliffe's property i.s distinguishable fron the effect on

is substantially the same as it is on all members of the public or

Advice Letter, No. A-90-017,' fn re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1; fn
re Owen (7976\ 2 FPPC Ops. 77. copies enclosed. )
The "public generally" is conprised of the entire
jurisdiction of the agency in question' (fn re Leqan, suDra.)
Because the agency in question is the ci.ty council and/or

entire city's population. Regulation 18703 also permits the
application of the 'rpublic generallyf exception when a decision
affects the official's 
interests in substantially the sane nanner
as it witl affect a significant segnent of the public.

facts indicating how, and whether, a specific decision concerning

segment, of thg city's residents uould required separate

sections.question_synthetic: May a Rancho Palos Verdes city council member and redevelopment agency member participate in decisions regarding landslide abatement projects and settlement implementation when he owns property in one of three affected landslide abatement districts?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The council member may participate in landslide abatement decisions if they will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his property interests, evaluated under the Political Reform Act's conflict of interest provisions and FPPC Regulation 18702.3.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; LLM: Document contains complete QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections. Analysis section references Regulation 18702.3 for evaluating materiality of financial effects on real property based on distance thresholds. Document marked as providing informal advice only (not formal written opinion). OCR quality good overall with minor character recognition issues in date and some section breaks.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: A member of the city's council and redevelopment agency lives in a landslide abatement district that is adjacent to two other landslide abatement districts. As a result of litigation settlements the council and agency will be making decisions to both implement the settlement agreements as well as to determine specific landslide abatement projects in the two adjacent districts. May the council and agency member participate in these decisions?

CONCLUSION: The council- and agency member may participate in decisions to implement the settlement and to undertake specific landslide abatement projects provided the decisions will not have a foreseeable material financial impact on his property interests.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission May 9′ 199■ Steven L. Dorsey RICHARDS, WATSON G GERSHON 333 South Hope Street, 38th Floor Los Angeles, CA 9007■―■469 Re: Your Request for Advice our Fi.le No. I -91- 2 01 Dear Mr. Dorsey: we have received your letter of March 28, L991, in r'rhich you seek advice on behalf of Douglas Hinchliffe, a member of the Rancho Palos Verdes city council and RedeveLopment Agency, regarding his duties and responsibilities under the conflict-of- i;i;;;;a'provisions of the potiticat Reform Act ("the Act").1 Pursuant to Regulation 18329 (copy enclosed) , we provide you with advice as Mr. Hinchliffe's authorized representat j' ve . The follolving advice is based upon the facts provided in your Ietter of March 2a, L99L and in a subsequent telephone conversation on April 4, L991. while it is understood from your Letter and the tel.ephone conversation that various governmental decisions are both occurring and forthcomj.ng, no specific pending decision was referenced in your Ietter. Therefore, the advj-ce provideo in this Letter is informal only.2 OUEST ION A member of the c j-ty's council and redevelopment agency lives in a landslide abatement district that is adjacent to two other Iandslide abatement districts. As a result of lj.tigation settlements the council and agency wil-I be making decisions to both implement the settlement agreements as weII as to determine 1 cor..nment code Sections 8looo-91015. AlL statutory references are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations section l8ooo, et seq. AII references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California code of Regulations. 2 Infoaral assistance does not provide the requestor with the j.romunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114,' Regulation 18329 (c) . ) 428 J Street, Suite 8O0 . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 95804-0807 . (9f 6)122'5660 File No. I-91-2o1 Page 2 specific landslide abatement prcjects in the two adjacent distrj,cts, May the council and agency member participate in these decisions? COIiCLUSION The council- and agency member may participate in decisions to implement the settlelflent and to undertake specific IandsLj.de abatement projects provided the decisions wiII not have a foreseeable material financiat impact on his property interests' FACTS Three landslides pertinent to this inquiry occurred in 1955 ( "Portugese Bendr') , L978 ("Abalone coverr) and 1980 ("KLondike canyon't) in or near the jurisdictj.onal boundaries of the city of Rancho Palos verdes (r'the city"), which was incorporated in 1973. The Portugese Bend landslide was found to have been created by the county of Los Angeles ("the county") as a resul-t of roadway construction. The geographic location of this landslide is between the other tvro: the Abalone Cove located to the west and the Klondike canyon located to the east. Following the Klondike Canyon tandslide in 1980, the city established two landslide abatement districts, the Abalone cove Landslide Abatement District and the Klondike canyon LandsLide Abatement District, to construct and maintain improvements designed to stop the landslides. The
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter concluding that Douglas Hinchliffe, a Rancho Palos Verdes city council and redevelopment agency member, may participate in decisions regarding landslide abatement projects and litigation settlement implementation, provided such decisions will not have a foreseeable material financial impact on his property interests under the Political Reform Act.
